content.text: Data Steward Roles and Accountability Model

Data governance without named accountability is a policy document, not a programme. An accountability model assigns specific responsibilities to specific roles so that every governance decision — classifying a new dataset, approving a document for the RAG knowledge base, retracting a document after an error, handling a data subject access request — has a clear owner who can be held to account. For Gen AI workloads, the accountability model must extend beyond the traditional data governance roles (CDO, DPO, domain stewards) to cover AI-specific responsibilities: who approves system prompts, who reviews model outputs, and who has authority to disable an application when a governance violation is detected.

This topic defines the stewardship roles, their accountability boundaries, the decision rights matrix (RACI), the data structures for tracking stewardship assignments in code, and the escalation paths that connect operational teams to executive accountability.

attrs.alt: Data steward roles and accountability model for GenAI governance

content.text: Sets data classification policy; approves risk appetite for Gen AI data use; accountable to regulators
Handles DSARs involving Gen AI; signs off on ROPA entries for Gen AI apps; reviews TIAs for cross-border transfers
Maintains classification taxonomy and handling rules; manages steward network; tracks governance KPIs
One per data domain (e.g. HR, Finance, Customer)
Decides classification for datasets in their domain; approves which domain data may be used in RAG; owns domain retention schedules
Approves system prompt content and changes; approves RAG knowledge base additions; owns application-level risk register
Reviews document ingestion requests; initiates document retraction; maintains sensitivity label accuracy of documents
Performs spot-checks on model outputs; escalates hallucinations or sensitivity violations; maintains false-negative register
Implements classification pipeline, lineage tracking, access control, audit log; no policy-setting authority
RACI Matrix for Key Gen AI Governance Decisions

The RACI matrix (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) prevents decision paralysis and ensures that governance actions happen at the right speed. 'Responsible' means doing the work; 'Accountable' means signing off and being held to account; only one role should be Accountable per decision.

Stewardship assignments must be machine-readable so that the governance tooling can enforce them — routing approval requests to the right person, sending alerts when a steward's SLA is breached, and generating the organisational map used in audit reports.
Approval Workflow for Knowledge Base Ingestion

Every document added to the RAG knowledge base requires explicit steward approval. The workflow has two mandatory sign-offs: the Domain Data Steward confirms that the document's sensitivity label is correct and that the domain policy permits the document to be used by this application; the AI System Steward approves the addition to their specific knowledge base. For documents labelled CONFIDENTIAL or REGULATED, the DPO is consulted.

from __future__ import annotations
import time, uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class ApprovalStatus(str, Enum):
    PENDING   = "pending"
    APPROVED  = "approved"
    REJECTED  = "rejected"
    ESCALATED = "escalated"

@dataclass
class IngestionApprovalRequest:
    request_id:      str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    doc_id:          str = ""
    source_uri:      str = ""
    sensitivity_label: str = "INTERNAL"
    target_kb_id:    str = ""               # knowledge base ID
    requester_email: str = ""
    submitted_utc:   float = field(default_factory=time.time)

    # Domain steward sign-off
    domain_scope_id: str = ""
    domain_decision:  ApprovalStatus = ApprovalStatus.PENDING
    domain_reviewer:  Optional[str] = None
    domain_notes:     str = ""
    domain_decided_utc: Optional[float] = None

    # AI system steward sign-off
    ai_system_id:    str = ""
    ai_decision:      ApprovalStatus = ApprovalStatus.PENDING
    ai_reviewer:      Optional[str] = None
    ai_notes:         str = ""
    ai_decided_utc:   Optional[float] = None

    # DPO consultation (required for CONFIDENTIAL+)
    dpo_required:    bool = False
    dpo_consulted:   bool = False
    dpo_notes:       str = ""

    def overall_status(self) -> ApprovalStatus:
        if self.domain_decision == ApprovalStatus.REJECTED:
            return ApprovalStatus.REJECTED
        if self.ai_decision == ApprovalStatus.REJECTED:
            return ApprovalStatus.REJECTED
        if self.dpo_required and not self.dpo_consulted:
            return ApprovalStatus.PENDING
        if (self.domain_decision == ApprovalStatus.APPROVED
                and self.ai_decision == ApprovalStatus.APPROVED):
            return ApprovalStatus.APPROVED
        return ApprovalStatus.PENDING

    def sla_hours_elapsed(self) -> float:
        return (time.time() - self.submitted_utc) / 3600

    def is_sla_breached(self, sla_hours: float = 48.0) -> bool:
        return (self.overall_status() == ApprovalStatus.PENDING
                and self.sla_hours_elapsed() > sla_hours)

Without SLAs, approval requests sit in stewards' inboxes indefinitely. The SLA framework sets maximum response times per label tier and triggers automatic escalation when a steward does not respond within the window.

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
from stewardship import StewardshipRegistry, StewardRole
from approval import IngestionApprovalRequest, ApprovalStatus

SLA_HOURS: dict[str, float] = {
    "PUBLIC": 120, "INTERNAL": 72, "RESTRICTED": 48,
    "CONFIDENTIAL": 24, "REGULATED": 4,
}

ESCALATION_CONTACTS: dict[str, list[str]] = {
    "PUBLIC":       ["governance.lead@example.com"],
    "INTERNAL":     ["governance.lead@example.com"],
    "RESTRICTED":   ["domain.head@example.com", "cdo@example.com"],
    "CONFIDENTIAL": ["dpo@example.com", "cdo@example.com"],
    "REGULATED":    ["dpo@example.com", "cdo@example.com", "legal@example.com"],
}

@dataclass
class EscalationEvent:
    request_id:      str
    label:           str
    hours_elapsed:   float
    escalation_to:   list[str]
    message:         str

def check_and_escalate(
    request: IngestionApprovalRequest,
    registry: StewardshipRegistry,
    notify_fn,   # callable(email: str, subject: str, body: str) -> None
) -> Optional[EscalationEvent]:
    if request.overall_status() != ApprovalStatus.PENDING:
        return None
    sla = SLA_HOURS.get(request.sensitivity_label, 72)
    elapsed = request.sla_hours_elapsed()
    if elapsed <= sla:
        return None

    contacts = ESCALATION_CONTACTS.get(request.sensitivity_label, [])
    event = EscalationEvent(
        request_id=request.request_id,
        label=request.sensitivity_label,
        hours_elapsed=round(elapsed, 1),
        escalation_to=contacts,
        message=(
            f"Ingestion approval request {request.request_id} for document "
            f"{request.doc_id} ({request.sensitivity_label}) has exceeded the "
            f"{sla}h SLA. Elapsed: {elapsed:.1f}h. Immediate action required."
        ),
    )
    for contact in contacts:
        notify_fn(contact, f"SLA Breach: Ingestion Approval {request.request_id}", event.message)
    return event

The Data Governance Lead tracks stewardship health through a small set of KPIs that are reported to the CDO monthly and to the Data Governance Council quarterly. These KPIs signal whether the programme is operating at the pace the business requires or whether steward capacity is a bottleneck.
Median hours from request submission to overall_status = APPROVED
Breached requests / total requests in period
Scopes with at least one unfilled required role / total scopes
Hours from retraction request to chunks_deindexed = True
Hours from DSAR receipt to lineage export delivered to DPO
Sampled responses reviewed by Output Reviewer / total responses (weekly sample)

A new steward who has not been onboarded to the governance tooling is a coverage gap even if their name is in the assignment registry. The following checklist must be completed within the first two weeks:
Read the Data Classification Policy and Handling Rules by Data Classification Tier
Complete the mandatory data governance training module (tracked in LMS)
Receive read access to the stewardship dashboard (approval queues and SLA metrics)
Be added to the governance Slack channel and distribution list
Shadow at least one existing steward through a complete approval cycle
Complete at least one supervised ingestion approval or retraction before handling independently
Confirm deputy assignment so the role has coverage during absences
Schedule first governance council attendance to understand the escalation path

The accountability model must enforce separation of duties: the person who requests ingestion of a document cannot be the same person who approves it, and the engineering team that implements the classification pipeline cannot also set the classification policy. Without these separations, the governance programme cannot provide assurance to regulators or auditors.
The requester of an ingestion approval must not be the AI System Steward for the same knowledge base — the steward must be a different person
The engineer who implements the sensitivity label classifier must not also be the Domain Data Steward who approves the classification taxonomy — policy and implementation must be separate
The Output Reviewer who spots checks responses must not be the same team that built the application — independent review requires no conflict of interest
The DPO must be independent of the business unit that operates the Gen AI application — this is a legal requirement under GDPR Article 38(3)

Practical implication: for small organisations where separation of duties is difficult due to headcount, document the conflict and obtain a compensating control — for example, a monthly review of all approvals by the Governance Lead where the same person served as both requester and approver in a different capacity.

The data stewardship accountability model for Gen AI extends the traditional CDO → Domain Steward hierarchy with three Gen AI-specific roles: the AI System Steward who owns application-level governance decisions, the Knowledge Base Steward who controls RAG document ingestion and retraction, and the Output Reviewer who maintains a spot-check programme on model responses. The RACI matrix assigns clear accountability for the nine most common governance decisions. The stewardship registry provides machine-readable role assignments that drive approval routing, SLA monitoring, and escalation. KPIs — approval cycle time, SLA breach rate, coverage gap rate, and DSAR response time — measure programme health. Separation of duties between requesters, approvers, implementers, and reviewers is the structural control that gives the programme its assurance value.
